sentences.text: [TONE]
My name is Nathan Nothman.
N-O-T-H-M-A-N.
Krakow, Poland.
Krakow is K-R-A-K-O-W, Poland.
I was going to school.
My mother had a store, fruit store.
My father was a plumber, a licensed plumber.
And I have three brothers and a sister.
My brother was-- I have an older brother and then older, a little older than me.
I was there, before the last, and then my sister, three brothers.
We all went to school.
We lived comfortably.
My mother had a store.
My father was working.
I helped my father, after school, to work with him, as a plumber.
He always told me that that would be very good trade in the future.
People like to build the houses, plumbing inside not outside, not in the hole.
And I agree with him, 100%.
Because now, I wind up to be a plumber, myself, in West Bloomfield, Michigan.
I went to school.
I belonged to Maccabee.
I play soccer with Maccabee.
I belonged to an organization.
We had a quiet life in Poland.
Krakow, Casimir, the king from Poland, 1492-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] which country is that?
Spain.
They send the Jews out from Spain.
They didn't like the Jews in Spain.
The king from Poland, Casimir, took the only Jews in sight right in Poland, because they need him.
They're educated.
They can do business.
They can run the government.
So he took all the Jewish people to Krakow.
And from this date-- we called him Kazimierz.
Where the Jews settle-- this might about five, six miles square in Krakow, that special place for the Jews, where the Jews live in it, was called a Kazimierz.
And that's where I lived.
I lived there, you know.
I was raised there.
I went to school.
Public school.
I went to public school.
And not too many years, because 1939, Germany come to Poland, and they stop all the Jewish to enter the school, all the businesses.
If any Jew is working for the government, they stopped them.
They just, they just told the Jews that they have no business in any government, in any school, lawyers, doctors.
But the doctors, I would say, because we have a Jewish hospital in Krakow, in Kazimierz.
So the Jewish doctors were working in a hospital just for the Jews.
But the Germans came in 1939, September.
January, 1940, they told us to go out of Poland and to go Krakow Podgorze.
This is after Krakow.
We passed the bridge.
The River Vistula separated Krakow and Podgorze.
So we went to the Podgorze.
What the German done, they evacuate all the Polish people and the Podgorze.
And we came in there.
And the Polish people took our places in Poland in the Kazimierz.
Let me ask you just some things before.
Do you remember how you found out that you had to leave?
Or did you get much warning?
Not the family, the Germans made a proclamation that every Jew must leave Krakow by March or April.
I forgot what month is  it.
They must be out of Krakow.
If they would be not out, they would be shot.
So from Krakow to Podgorze, I would say some, maybe a mile away, some maybe 2 miles away, some maybe 3 miles away, that's it.
Because we were very close to the river, Vistula.
So after we crossed the bridge, we were already not  in Krakow.
We were in Podgorze.
Walked there.
Jews could not drive a streetcar.
Jews couldn't have no cars, no nothing.
There was no streetcar for Jews.
Yes, we took-- because we took as much as we can, almost everything from the Krakow, Kazimierz, to ghetto Plaszow.
No, closed, completely closed.
So we went to ghetto.
My father worked a lot for the Nazi as a plumber.
And I worked, too, with them, and my brother.
My older brother didn't like it, what the German done at Kristallnacht, 1938.
So my brother said to my parents, let's go to Russia.
We have a better chance to be survive-- to survive the Germany, Germans.
Let's go to Russia.
But my father said, no, no.
I know Germans because they are this and this.
But my brother said, no, I'm going out.
So he went to Russia.
You were able to leave the ghetto?
Yes, not ghetto-- in Krakow, 1939, before, before we went to ghetto.
But my brother said, no.
He went.
And he survived the war.
And that means my father, my brother, my sister, my mother, and myself, we were in the ghetto, five of us.
We were in the ghetto.
So my mother had a store, fruit store like we have in Krakow.
My father was a plumber.
But the Germans, right away, took my father to  work, because they didn't pay too much.
They didn't pay at all, nothing.
So we get passes to get out of ghetto.
So that means we got passes to get out and to walk.
If the job is 5 miles, we walk 5 miles, or 6 miles  or 3 miles.
But we walk, and we have a bicycle.
And in the bicycle, we have a lot of tools, like [INAUDIBLE] and ice cream, like they're selling ice cream?
And we have the tools in the bicycle.
And we always try to push the bicycle.
Hill up, we're supposed to push up.
But if it was a flat road, we could pedal that-- the bicycle with a big box in front of us.
So my father worked for the Nazi.
Just, Not just my father, there was maybe six, seven of them.
There was a carpenter.
There was an electrician.
There was a bricklayer.
There was a heating man.
Everybody of us was a Jew.
Do you remember what kind of work they were?
What they done, they went to a big building and throw the Polish people out, everything out, throw them out.
And they told us, I want you to remodel.
Change the walls.
Change the rooms, you know, anything that was supposed to be done.
I want you to put a bathroom in it, a toilet in that room, in this room.
And repair this and change this.
You know, so everybody work for the, all the tradesmen, what I just pronounced, worked for the Nazis.
That's when my father knew Goeth.
Goeth?
He was the commander from the ghetto.
And my father is a Berliner, so he spoke the language very good.
And there was a lot of discussion.
Like I remember once, when my father went to the building with us, and the Germans talked with German with my father.
He asked my father, how long would the job take?
He said it would take two weeks.
No, it would take one week.
And my father said-- I remember, in German-- this is [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], this is impossible.
So the [?
German ?] said, I'll take a gun, and I'll blow your head if you tell me once that this is impossible.
I have one week.
So my father went to ghetto and got more people so we can finish in one week.
And we worked day and night.
Because when he said kill, it's kill.
He was this ruthless killer from Austria, Vienna, that I ever saw in my life.
And later, I will explain why.
This is unbelievable when a man can kill people without ever hesitating, with nothing, like they would be just pieces of wax, of paper or rats or anything.
So we were in the ghetto.
We worked.
My father worked.
After my father finished that work in ghetto, 1942, two Polish inspectors came in, the Gestapo, to my father.
And I was there in the house, too.
Are you named Shimon Nothman?
Yes.
You are under arrest.
They chain him, and then took him away.
We never saw him again.
He went to Auschwitz.
Never saw him again.
Oh, about four months later, they came for my brother, older from me, and the same thing.
What was your older brother's name?
Lolak.
Lolak Nothman.
And they took him.
And I was rebelling.
I was rebelling against-- I said to my brother, run, hide.
But I don't know, every person is different.
Because we could hide.
But where would you hide?
The only place to run away, through the ghetto, out to the wall, and go to the forest.
But it was maybe too late for him, or he didn't realize what's going on.
By the time we say something, he already was handcuffed, and he was out.
So my mother, my sister, and myself left.
We three leftover in that [INAUDIBLE], in that ghetto.
In the ghetto, we have policemen watching the entrance.
There was a one or two or three entrances coming to the ghetto.
The Polish policeman, they were the worst.
Anybody brought a bread or let me say, oh, you brought some salami, if you can buy-- you know, when we got a pass going out of the ghetto, we're in Krakow.
We would  pass the bridge.
We were already in Krakow.
So you can buy something.
You know, some farmer may want to sell something, or you trade, or you bring some clothes with you when you go out.
You trade with the farmers or with the people.
They want to trade.
They need the shoe, or they need the-- you know.
So when I, let me say, I brought something in.
I brought half a pound of butter.
So the police said, what do you have there?
I have a little butter.
No, you cannot do it.
You cannot have it.
Give  it away.
I said, it's for me.
I'm hungry.
I'm starving.
Nothing.
They were very, very vicious.
Police, there was not a German in it.
There was-- most of them, they were 99% Polish policeman.
And they were terrible.
And they took everything away.
So--
Yes.
I remember, when I was young, and I belonged to an organization, we call them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], people who are anti-Semites, not just for Jews, you know.
Like Hitler say, only the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the clean white people only.
And not just white, the German blood, no else can be adopted to us.
In the Polish side, the same thing, only that the people who are clean, clean blood from Poland, something like this.
And they were anti-Semites.
They hated Jews.
They hated not just the Jews, the Gypsies.
They were against non-believers and against the communists.
You  know, they all-- they all just fought for the Polish.
So I remember, once, they come with truckloads, right by the-- not far from the Kazimierz, about half a mile.
And we got a tip-off that they would come.
And a  lot of organizations-- like I was, at the time, maybe 11 years old  or 12.
We took a broomstick.
We hide in the forest.
There's a small, tiny forest there, between Kazimierz, in between that Polish people, where they live.
And where they come in, we start to swing and beat with the sticks them, very hard, very hard.
Even policemen were there, and they turned away a little bit.
Because they were with us.
And I remembered, there were a lot of ambulance came in, you know.
And nobody was arrested.
Nobody said nothing.
And that was forgotten.
And they never come again to Krakow, big city.
So I don't remember which year was it, but I figure 1936.
Something like this, I can't remember.
If you were 11, '36 or '37.
11  years or something.
Yes, '36, something, I remember.
And we started to fight with them.
But for most of the time, there was anti-Semites.
But in the Kazimierz, there were Jews living.
So we were in the center.
So we didn't see like somebody come in and try to beat somebody dead, because there was the Jews.
There were 120,000, maybe, or something.
[INAUDIBLE].
Were you from-- oh, excuse me.
Were you from a religious family?
My mother was religious.
But I was just to God and to people, most to people.
I was good.
I didn't hurt nobody.
And I wanted the same thing from people, to respect me.
But we were not religious.
Myself, my father was not.
My mother, yes.
We would light a candle.
We went to schul.
I went to schul, too.
[INAUDIBLE].
They had bar mitzvah.
I remember that.
But when I like ham, I eat ham.
It tastes good, so I ate  it.
It's delicious.
Ooh, sure, Polish kielbasa, oh, so many of them, everywhere, everyplace.
So it was good.
It tastes good, so I, yeah.
But that was a quiet life.
But when the Germans come in, 1939.
I remember, they caught a Jewish person, maybe about 60 years old, and he have a beard.
So they took a salami, non-kosher salami, and they tried to force  it into his mouth.
Eat it.
So he said, I wouldn't eat.
And they tried to-- and they took the scissors and shaved his beard.
He was crying.
They forced the salami.
So I said to the German, give me.
I will eat that for you.
No, not you.
So he wouldn't eat.
He wouldn't even open the mouth.
So they give me the salami.
I said, they taste good.
You got more?
But they came in and tried to take advantage on the old people who cannot defend themselves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some beat them up, a little bit, too.
In '39, they make fun of them, how they look.
How they-- you know.
And most of the time, they know they're Jewish, especially with that orthodox.
They will not eat salami, what is non-kosher, especially ham and bacon.
Oh, that's terrible.
And a lot of occasions, they came in, a lot of times, to catch people to work.
A truck, two trucks came in.
And there is a big place, marketplace.
[INAUDIBLE] the marketplace, a lot of times, I was inside there, in the market.
But they couldn't catch me, because I was too fast.
I was playing soccer, and I could run like a rabbit.
And I went to the building.
By the time I was in the building, I wind up half a mile away, because I jumped the fences, inside the building.
Because the buildings were together, connected together.
So we can jump each, you know-- wind up-- I come to that building, and I can wind up half a mile away.
So once, I didn't have no chase.
They caught me, because I was right middle-- right by the machine gun.
So we work, we went there to work for them, for two days, three days, sometimes a week.
And they brought us back to the ghetto after the job was finished, unloading coal.
They came-- a train came, commercial trains with coal, thousands, thousands of tons of them.
So we're supposed to unload it, or we're supposed to clean the snow for them.
I remember, I was working in kranken lazarette.
That is a hospital for sick people.
I was working there, in ghetto, too.
And there was the war broke off with Russia.
And they took us in.
They gave us uniforms.
And we were supposed to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That means I was supposed to disinfect myself, the whole body.
There was a big barrel there.
There was a big barrel there outside.
We were supposed to put a hand in it and smear all the body, under the arm, everywhere, everywhere, and then take a shower and put the uniform.
And we were the Red Cross, from the hospital, on trucks.
Went to the station where the commercial train came in.
And I saw the Germans, frozen legs, frozen noses, frozen toes, everything.
So they thought that we were German.
So they said to us, in German, we're not going to the Eastern Front.
We're not going.
The Eastern Front is Russia.
And we were not going.
It's terrible.
It's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
It's, it's-- but we didn't say nothing, because we don't want to be, you know, discuss with them.
So we took them to that-- on the trucks, took them to the hospital.
And every time, where we took them, they were completely in-- completely lice.
When you're sitting on a train for four or five days, and you sit on the front for maybe a week, you don't wash yourself.
So now, we're supposed to throw the uniform out.
Do the same thing.
Take a shower, new uniform, their hair.
They shave us hair, maybe half an inch.
Hair was just half an inch.
And we worked there for three or four weeks.
And that was when they come from the Eastern Front.
June, June, 1942.
We were in the ghetto.
They came to the ghetto, and they caught us.
You know.
Sometimes they caught us.
Or sometimes they say to the, to the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Jewish authority, because there was a like an authority.
That when they wanted some people, they go to the Jewish people and said, listen, we want so many people, so many people.
You should bring-- you should tell the people to be there.
We'll pick the people up with the trucks or so on.
So there was a policeman.
There was, you know, a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like a government, you know, but only for the Germans.
When they wanted something, they didn't went to us, they went to the government.
So a lot of times, they told me, you must come here.
You must be in that 2 o'clock or 1 o'clock or any time they give me, in the morning.
You'll go for a few days, there and there and  there.
So they took us a lot of times.
That's where they took me to the kranken lazarette in 1942, in June or July.
I cannot remember when.
That's why we were there for a few weeks there.
And my mother didn't know about it.
So they brought me back to the ghetto, Podgorze ghetto.
And we worked a lot of places.
And we worked, for 1941, for Schindler, Schindler.
We worked him, because he got a factory.
So we went there to build, to repair, to fix.
So back and forth, back and forth, we went there.
And I was not there, because he got his own people there.
I wind up in the ghetto.
And after 1943, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1942, Goeth, anybody who didn't have a seal from him-- and the Germans were looking for houses.
Show me your pass.
You got a seal?
You stay.
You don't have-- you have no seal, you get.
So a lot of times, they caught me without the seal.
So they took me to the station there.
But my father knew exactly what's going on.
He told me, I should take a channel lock and put my arm, with a rubber-band, tight, small channel lock, very sharp.
Chain lock?
Channel lock, you know, like you're cutting?
Like you want to cut something with a heavy, with a sharp thing.
So I ask him why?
You will see it.
If they ever take you in a, in a commercial train, they put barbed wire on it.
And you cannot escape.
You cannot take the clothes and to tear the wire.
So you know, I was a mechanic already.
I worked for my father.
So I understand.
So they took me once, with my neighbor.
He did have no pass, so they took him, with my girlfriend.
Naturally, she was young.
She was about-- what is it? 1942, I was maybe about 13, 12? 13, yeah? '42, 12? 12, 13, 14? 17!
Right, 17.
And I found out that they were with me.
And I found out when we were walking to the train, I said, wait  a minute.
Let's stick together.
So we went to the commercial train.
So I told-- there was a brother, older, with me.
And there was a mother, father, and another, [?
Rifkila ?].
This is that girl.
Well, we lived together.
We were in the same building.
We were almost raised together.
So I told them, listen, we're not going to Auschwitz.
We know we're going to Auschwitz.
Remember, in Auschwitz, there's no chance.
And I was joking.
You know why I don't like to go to Auschwitz?
Because they don't serve breakfast in the morning.
You know, just like a joke.
And I said, we're going out.
We're jumping out.
I said, how can we do it?
Look at the wire.
Don't worry about the wire.
And when there was a little dark, I went on the shoulder, and I cut the wire.
I cut the wire straight and let it stay.
So I cut it like an L-shape, like a U-shape.
And the wire was straight.
And then we let it go.
After the train pulled out, I pulled the wire  up, all the way up.
And I said, I'm going out.
So he said to me, now-- so he said to his father, listen, father, I am not going to Auschwitz.
I want to go with Nate.
And my girlfriend said, I'm going with Nate.
But you know, a father is the boss, especially about the orthodox.
And she was crying to her mother.
So the son said, if you don't give me permission, I'm going.
And I'm going with Nate.
And I'm going, too.
The mother said, go ahead, save yourself.
And the father was praying, praying, and that thing.
And you know, I said to him, that doesn't help.
But we couldn't take him, because it's impossible.
If he would jump from the train, he would be killed.
So now, I went first out.
Then [?
Rifkila ?], Regina-- Regina, that's the name, you  know, let's put it that way.
Regina went up.
And I said to her, I'm, I'm jumping first.
We went down to the [INAUDIBLE] to lowest, lowest steps, what can we get from the commercial train.
And I told her, when you jump, you jump forwards.
And you try to roll.
So I show how to-- I went first.
I got hurt.
She was in the middle.
And he was the last.
So I said, we will come together to Regina.
After we jump in, we will come together.
So we'll see what happened with Regina.
But she was a little hurt.
And what happened, we walked all night.
We went to the ghetto.
How to ghetto?
I took the brick from the ghetto, bricks, notched bricks.
So I took a hammer, chisel, knock it nice, so nobody would see it.
And I pushed the brick back.
So when I took the brick and tossed it the other side, we went for the bricks, over.
We went down, we would put the bricks back, like nothing happened.
And that's where we went back to the ghetto.
Had you gone in and out of the ghetto?
Oh, yes, oh yes, a lot of times.
I couldn't be boxed.
They couldn't box me in.
Because I was-- I speak Polish.
I was blond.
And I look-- I was even-- I was in a movie.
I went to the movie, even that, to see what movie.
I saw German Polish war, 1939, a World War II movie.
And I was sitting like the Germans and this and this.
I dressed myself nice, with my brother, blond, too.
We speak perfect Polish.
Like, you know.
And we went on the streetcar, sat on the streetcar.
And they jump, jumped.
Before the streetcar went to the ghetto, we'd jump out and went to the, the-- what are you calling-- the wall back to the ghetto, twice, three, four, five times.
So I went back and forth.
I-- I didn't worry about that.
We played soccer in that, in the ghetto, too, you know?
Little boys, you know, we found a place.
We found [INAUDIBLE] a ball, so we played.
But the most thing, what is it?
That they came in, when the people didn't have no pass, they were killing, killing so many people, killing, shooting so many people.
Sometimes there was the people who were very old.
He couldn't walk.
So they shot him right in a bed, [? '42, ?] in the bed.
In the bed.
People couldn't walk, somebody have arthritis or somebody have multiple sclerosis.
That's what we know now.
At that time, we didn't know about this.
We didn't know about multiple sclerosis.
But now, we know about it.
So they shot him in the bed.
In the ghetto, yes.
Yes, a lot of people die in the ghetto, a lot of people.
Sure, we saw that.
We took a horse and buggy.
We took the dead body on a horse and buggy.
Everybody can see it, sure.
And lot of people died, too, from starvation, not enough food.
Yes.
Yes.
For some, yes, and for some, no.
We had bread.
With that bread what they give us, we can build, we can put the bricks.
That bread was like a clay.
That was not white.
That was a brown, brown, dark brown.
It was terrible.
Because they didn't put no wheat, no nothing, just the lowest what can ever be.
That's what the horses probably eat, because we never saw white bread or any even close to white, just dark brown bread.
